coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent green and red emissions from boron-doped graphene quantum dots under blue light illumination
coredata.dc:creator: Juang, Ruey-Shin
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Kao, Chih-Peng
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
Fu, Chun-Chieh
Liu, Shou-Hsuan
Gu, Siyong
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-doped GQDs, average particle size of ∼4 nm) are synthesized via one-pot solvothermal route in the presence of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and boric acid. The chemical ratio of the boric acid to TNP precursor is identified as a crucial factor in modulating the B-dopant concentration within the GQD lattices. The GQD suspensions maintain uniform dispersion over extended operation thanks to the NO2 groups formed in the GQD edge sites promoting high dispersivity in polar and nonpolar solvents. The B-doped GQD suspensions are homogeneously stored in n-hexane and propylene glycol methyl ether acetate, enabling highly fluorescent green and red emission, respectively. Under the blue-light illumination, the green emission takes place at ca. 541 nm with a narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 31.7 nm, whereas the red emission centers at ca. 617 nm with a FWHM of 42.5 nm. Through adjusting the concentration of B-dopant, the quantum yield of green and red fluorescence reached as high as 99.8 and 17.1%, respectively. Accordingly, the novel solvothermal synthesis route developed in this work provides the essential framework for engineering B-doped GQDs with tunable optical properties for high-performance biosensors and biomedical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron doping
Graphene quantum dots
Red emission
High quantum yield
Hydrothermal synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical and PL characterization of B-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram illustrating the solvothermal synthesis of B-doped GQD samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HR-TEM micrographs of different GQD samples: (a,d) B-GQD-13, (b,e) B-GQD-12, and (c,f) B-GQD-11. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Variation of B/C atomic ratio with the chemical ratio of boric acid to TNP. XPS spectra of pristine GQD sample: (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of B-GQD-13 sample: (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) B 1s, and (d) O 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photographs of (a) GQD, (b) B-GQD-13, (c) B-GQD-12, and (d) B-GQD-11 suspensions under sunlight and UV (wavelength: 360 nm) illumination. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photographs of (a) different B-GQD-13 suspensions in syringe and (b) the “CGU” patterns injected by the B-GQD-13 suspension onto paper: before and after under UV illumination. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra, (b) PL excitation spectra, and PL emission spectra of B-GQD-13 suspension in (c) n-hexane and (d) PGMEA excited at different wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Maximal wavelength (MW) and full-width half height as function of different GQD suspensions and (b) variation of quantum yield with the chemical ratio of boric acid to TNP precursor under blue-light illumination. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent green and red emissions from boron-doped graphene quantum dots under blue light illumination

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-doped GQDs, average particle size of ∼4 nm) are synthesized via one-pot solvothermal route in the presence of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and boric acid. The chemical ratio of the boric acid to TNP precursor is identified as a crucial factor in modulating the B-dopant concentration within the GQD lattices. The GQD suspensions maintain uniform dispersion over extended operation thanks to the NO2 groups formed in the GQD edge sites promoting high dispersivity in polar and nonpolar solvents. The B-doped GQD suspensions are homogeneously stored in n-hexane and propylene glycol methyl ether acetate, enabling highly fluorescent green and red emission, respectively. Under the blue-light illumination, the green emission takes place at ca. 541 nm with a narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 31.7 nm, whereas the red emission centers at ca. 617 nm with a FWHM of 42.5 nm. Through adjusting the concentration of B-dopant, the quantum yield of green and red fluorescence reached as high as 99.8 and 17.1%, respectively. Accordingly, the novel solvothermal synthesis route developed in this work provides the essential framework for engineering B-doped GQDs with tunable optical properties for high-performance biosensors and biomedical devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are promising spherical/spheroidal nanomaterials (average diameter 2–20 nm) with numerous potential applications for optical devices [1]. In particular, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a promising class of highly luminescent nanomaterials. The major attributes of GQDs include low cytotoxicity, tunable emission, ease of production, and superior resistance to photobleaching [2–4]. However, low quantum yield (QY) along with weak red emission (compared to those of commercially available semiconductor-based QDs (e.g., CdTe)) are the major challenges yet to be addressed for wide-spread utilization of GQDs as the luminescent materials [5].

The red-emissive QDs have priorly been used for photodetection and have found several applications in biomedical and energy devices [6–8]. Thanks to excellent photostability and biocompatibility, red-emissive GQDs are considered an ideal bio-luminescent probe for bio-imaging. Accordingly, it is highly desirable to develop advanced synthesis techniques for preparing GQDs with highly luminescent red emission and high QY capabilities. Doping GQDs with heteroatoms (e.g., O, N, B, S, etc.) has been demonstrated to be an effective strategy for modulating their intrinsic electronic, luminescence, and reactive properties [9–12].

Nitrogen, the first dopant introduced within the graphene matrix, has been extensively exploited for inducing n-type conductivity [13]. Boron is another promising dopant material that compared to nitrogen, has less electronegativity (even less than carbon; 2.04 for B versus 2.55 for C [14]), and thus induces p-type conductivity in the graphene structure [13]. Several prior efforts have reported on synthesizing B-doped GQDs (B-GQDs) and B-doped carbon nanomaterials including hydrothermal carbonization of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and borax in NaOH [14], hydrothermal synthesis of glucose + boric acid [15], melamine + boric acid [16], and ammonium citrate + bis(pinacolato) diboron [17], thermal pyrolysis of GQDs + boric acid mixture [18], hydrothermal cutting of B-doped graphene using a strong oxidizing acid [19], microwave-assisted reaction of boron carbide and polyethylene glycol [20], electrochemical cutting of boracic graphite [21], and high temperature microwave digestion of graphene oxide in Na2B4O7 solution [22]. The before mentioned techniques enable synthesizing B-GQDs with tunable electronic properties. However, to the best of our knowledge, no prior work has focused on preparing B-GQDs, capable of emitting highly fluorescent red emission under the blue-light illumination.

In this work, we have developed one-pot solvothermal method for synthesizing B-GQDs in organic media using oil soluble TNP and boric acid as the nitrogen/carbon and boron sources, respectively. The TNP precursor was chosen since by solvothermal molecular fusion, hydrophobic graphene-like core of the resulting B-GQDs is directly exposed to nonpolar organic media while NO2 groups are arranged in their edges, leading to high solubility in a wide range of polar and/or nonpolar solvents (excluding water) [23]. The synthesis method demonstrated in this work is capable of producing luminescent B-GQDs with ultra-high QY (∼100%) under the blue-light illumination. We have also explored the influence of B-dopant concentration on the PL response of B-GQDs dispersed within organic solvents, n-hexane and propylene glycol methyl ether acetate (PGMEA). We found that the chemical ratio of TNP to boric acid is a crucial factor influencing the photoluminescence (PL) performance of the B-GQD samples.

To synthesize B-GQDs, first, the TNP precursor was chemically modified through the nitration of pyrene in concentrated nitric acid [23,24]. The nitration process was moderately modified where the pyrene (2 g) was homogeneously mixed in 240 mL concentrated HNO3 (16 N) at 80 °C for 18 h using a magnetic bar at 150 rpm. Followingly, the resulting mixture was cooled down to the ambient temperature and diluted with distilled water (2 L). The diluted solution was continuously stirred at room temperature for 24 h to ensure the presence of NOx groups attached to TNP molecules. Finally, the mixture was filtered and subsequently dried at 60 °C for 24 h to produce the TNP precursor.

The as-prepared TNP (2 g) powder and boric acid (1 g) was dispersed in 50 mL dimethylformamide (DMF, boiling point: 153 °C) using an ultrasonic bath at ambient temperature for 0.5 h. The homogeneous solution was placed in a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (120 mL). The solvothermal synthesis process was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h and then cooled down to room temperature. The suspension containing organic-soluble B-GQDs were filtered through a microporous membrane (pore size: 0.22 μm) to remove any traces of insoluble impurities. Finally, the suspension was dried in a rotary evaporator at 45 °C in a vacuum. To further elaborate on the influence of boric acid on the performance of B-GQDs, the solvothermal method was employed for synthesizing GQDs without any boric acid. The corresponding samples containing no boric acid are denoted as pristine GQD throughout the entire paper. The B-GQD samples with different B-dopant concentrations were also prepared where the chemical ratios of boric acid to TNP were set at 1:3, 1:2, and 1:1 in w/w, labeled as B-GQD-13, B-GQD-12, and B-GQD-11, respectively. Schematic diagram illustrating the solvothermal synthesis of B-doped GQD clusters is provided in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical and PL characterization of B-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The microstructure of B-GQD samples were inspected using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Talos F200s). Fourier-transform IR (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on Nicolet 380 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fison VG ESCA210) equipped with Mg-Kα radiation emitter, was used to characterize the chemical composition of B-GQD samples. The C 1s, N 1s, B 1s, and O 1s spectra were analyzed using a non-linear least squares fitting algorithm with a symmetric Gaussian function developed in-house. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using an Agilent Cary 60 spectrometer, in which the wavelength scan rate was set at 60 nm/min. The B-GQD suspensions (concentration: 100 mg/L) were ultrasonically vibrated in distilled water bath at ambient temperature for 0.5 h to ensure a homogeneous dispersion. The resulting B-GQD suspensions were very stable for several months. The PL emission spectra of each suspension were acquired using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) at different wavelengths including 360, 380, 400, 420, and 450 nm. The QY values of the suspensions were measured with respect to the standard Coumarin reference (C9H6O2, molecular weight: 146; QY: 73% at 373 nm excitation). The QY value of each GQD suspension was subsequently quantified using the following equation [10].
(1) QY = QYr × [(PL area/A)s / (PL area/A)r] × Փ s 2/Փ r 2

Here ‘s’ and ‘r’ subscripts represent the GQD sample and the reference (i.e., Coumarin), respectively. Also, Փ is used for quantifying the reflective index of the solvent, and ‘PL area’ and A represents the PL emission spectral area and absorbance value, respectively.

HR-TEM micrograph of pristine GQD sample, as illustrated in the
Electronic
Supporting
Information
(see Fig. S1), reveals a homogeneous dispersion of nanodots. As shown in Fig. S1, the as-prepared pristine GQD samples have spherical shape with an average diameter of ∼4.0 nm. We also observe a well-ordered lattice fringes with an average spacing distance of ca. 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite, confirming the formation of a graphite-like structure [25]. Fig. 2 shows HR-TEM micrographs of different B-doped GQDs including B-GQD-13, B-GQD-12, and B-GQD-11 samples with low and high magnifications. According to Fig. 2, all samples maintain their original spherical-like structure even after incorporating the B-dopant into the internal morphology. The lattice fringes of all B-doped GQD samples can be viewed along the (100) facet of graphite. However, the lattice fringes are localized and dominate partial domains, resulting in the development of grain boundaries along with several defects within the lattice structure.

Indeed, boron doping alters the lattice arrangement due to substitutional or interstitial replacement on the basal planes or at the edge sites of GQDs. The particle size distributions of all GQD samples, as shown in Fig. S2, reveal a quasi-Gaussian distribution centering at 3–5 nm. The average particle size of the pristine GQD, B-GQD-13, B-GQD-12, and B-GQD-11 samples were estimated to be 4.0, 3.9, 3.8, and 4.0 nm, respectively. Therefore, the influence of B doping on both the particle shape and particle size is insignificant.

FT-IR spectra, as shown in Fig. S3, were obtained to confirm the presence of surface functional groups on pristine GQD and B-GQD-11 samples. First, one band in the range of 1150–1300 cm−1 corresponds to C–O stretch of –COOH, indicating the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the GQD samples [26–28]. Second, one transmittance peak appears at approximately 1710 cm−1, mainly originated from the existence of CN or CO functional groups [29,30]. As to B-GQD-11 sample, the FT-IR peaks at 1394 and 1103 cm−1 can be attributed to B–O and C–B stretching, respectively [31]. The FT-IR results confirmed that N- and B-containing functional groups were formed through the solvothermal synthesis and successfully doped into the skeleton of the GQD samples.

XPS measurements were used to inspect the dopant concentration and distribution of surface functionalities induced by the introduction of boron dopants. Based on the survey-scan XPS spectra (see Fig. S4), pristine GQD sample possesses three main elements: C 1s (ca. 282–288 eV), N 1s (ca. 399–408 eV), and O 1s (ca. 531–536 eV) [32,33], whereas an additional B 1s peak at ca. 190–194 eV is detected for all B-doped GQDs [8]. The B/C atomic ratio as an increasing function of the chemical ratio of boric acid to TNP is illustrated in Fig. 3 (a) where the B/C ratio increases from 1.16 at.% (B-GQD-13) to 39.2 at.% (B-GQD-11). This finding reveals that the chemical ratio of the boric acid to TNP can be tuned for controlling the dopant concentration within the GQD lattices.

Fig. 3(b)‒3(d) present high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks of the pristine GQD samples, deconvoluted by a multiple Gaussian function. Herein the C 1s spectrum can be decomposed into five peaks at ca. 284.5 eV (CC/C–C), ca. 286.0 eV (C–N/C–OH), ca. 286.8 eV (CO), referring to different bonding types to the C atoms [33,34]. The presence of oxygen functionalities indicates that the edges of sp2 graphite-like lattices are decorated by various functional groups including the C–N and C–O groups. The N 1s spectrum was decomposed into three major peaks assignable to pyridinic/pyrrolic N, graphitic N (ca. 401.1 eV), and NO2 (ca. 406.5 eV) [23]. The pyridinic/pyrrolic N has been identified to be bonded with the single-crystalline GQD lattice most probably at the edge sites rather than at the basal plane sites [35–37]. The O 1s spectrum was composed of two main peaks at ca. 531.8 and ca. 533.5 eV, assigned to the CO and C–O/C–NO2 bonds, respectively [23]. Accordingly, the pristine GQDs contain not only lattice N atoms but also NO2 groups residing at the edge sites.

High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, B 1s, and O 1s peaks of B-GQD-13 samples are provided in Fig. 4 (a)‒4(d). Compared to the pristine GQDs, one additional group (i.e., C–B at ca. 283.9 eV [38,39]) can be observed from the C 1s peak, indicating the presence of B dopants within the graphite-like lattice. The N 1s spectrum was composed of three components including the N–B bond (ca. 398.3 eV), pyridinic/pyrrolic N (ca. 399.6–400.2 eV), graphitic N (ca. 401.4 eV), and NO2 (ca. 406.5 eV) [23,40,41]. The B 1s spectrum of B-GQD-13 shows three peaks located at ∼189.2, 189.8, 190.6 and 191.9 eV, corresponding to B–N, B–C, BC2O, and BCO2 bands, respectively (see Fig. 4(c)) [42,43]. Therefore, it can be inferred that the doping of B atoms into the skeleton of GQDs has been performed successfully.

It is important to note that the intense peak at ca. 190.6 eV can be assigned to the structure of B atoms bonding to the C and O atoms (BC2O), indicating the presence of a large fraction of boron in the form of epoxy. The signal at ca. 191.9 eV reveals that B atoms are surrounded by C and O atoms (BCO2), confirming the existence of boronic acid group within the B-GQD-13 sample [14,44]. Based on the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (see Fig. 4(d)), the presence of the CO and C–O/B–O/C–NO2 bonds (at ca. 531.8 and ca. 533.5 eV, respectively) can be identified within the GQD molecular structure [14,23,45]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the B atoms are arranged with four different bonding structures (i.e., N–B, C–B, BC2O, and BCO2) inserted into or covered over the N-doped graphene network of GQDs.

Analyzing the XPS data associated with the B-GQD-12 and B-GQD-11 samples (see Figs. S5 and S6) reveals that the B 1s peaks are dominated by the B–N, B–C, BC2O, and BCO2 species. For the B-GQD-12 and B-GQD-11 samples, the B/C atomic ratio increases to 6.5 and 39.2 at.%, respectively; thus, the dominating effect of the BC2O and BCO2 compounds intensifies with increased dopant concentration. This reveals that the C and B atoms tend to exist as pairs upon enhancing the doping content. Indeed, the degree of B-doping plays a substantial role influencing the QY and PL response in different solvents.

Fig. 5 includes the photographs of all GQD suspensions in different solvents including the distilled water, n-hexane, and PGMEA under sunlight and UV irradiation. Under UV illumination, we observe that the pristine GQD suspension emits blue, green, and red fluorescence in water, n-hexane, and PGMEA, respectively. Similarly, the B-doped GQD samples also illuminate an identical color scheme but with different intensities. This observation reveals that the PL response from the B-doped GQD suspensions is solvent-dependent and the B-dopant concentration can be adjusted for tuning the PL intensity.

To explore the long-term stability of the suspension, the solutions containing GQDs were stored for several months. Followingly, the sediments were observed in the bottom of the GQD suspensions in water since the functionalized GQDs had a poor dispersion in water. In contrast, the suspensions in n-hexane and PGMEA displayed no sign of any sediment due to the existence of NO2 groups residing on the GQD edges, resulting in high dispersivity in both nonpolar and polar solvents [23]. As illustrated in Fig. 5, both B-GQD-13 and B-GQD-12 suspension in n-hexane emit fluorescent green light under the sunlight illumination, implying high intense PL response due to the B doping. To confirm the efficacy of the B-doped GQDs as high-performance imaging probes, three syringes filled with the B-GQD-13 suspensions in water, n-hexane, and PGMEA were injected into a typical photocopy paper, as shown in Fig. 6 . Upon drying, the injected label, “CGU”, on the paper emits green, red, and blue patterns (on-demand) for each letter under the UV illumination. This capability can be immensely beneficial for the PL labeling employed in light emitting, anti-counterfeiting, bio-imaging, and disease-detection devices [12,46,47].

UV–vis absorption spectra of B-doped GQD suspensions in n-hexane and PGMEA are illustrated in Fig. 7 (a). The B-GQD-13 suspension in n-hexane consists of three absorption regions including: (i) 200–280 nm (π→π∗ transition of CC of sp2 domain in sp3 C matrix), (ii) 300–400 nm (n→π∗ transition of CO and CN), and (iii) 400–600 nm (n→π∗ transitions of non-bonding electrons of adatoms). The adsorption of adatoms (e.g., B–C, C–O, B–O, C–NO2, N–B, etc.) commonly alters the surface structure and can even generate additional edge sites facilitating the absorption [48–50].

Considering the first two absorption regions (i.e., (i) and (ii)) the absorbance spectra is almost identical at various wavelengths for the suspension in n-hexane and PGMEA. However, the absorption edge (i.e., region (iii)) in PGMEA shifts from 540 to 610 nm, as compared to the absorption spectrum recorded for the B-doped GQD suspension in n-hexane. It is well established that the n-hexane is a nonpolar solvent with an ultralow relative polarity (∼0.009); whereas PGMEA is a hydrophilic solvent with high solubility in water (∼19.8 g/100 mL water at 25 °C). Thus, the interfacial structure between the solvent molecules and the heterogeneous B-doped GQD surface (e.g., localized defect, adatom decoration, chemical bonding) play a critical role affecting the dispersion extent of the GQD nanoparticles. Indeed, the physicochemical properties of the solvent directly influences the fluorescent performance of GQDs via altering the dispersion level along with the surface state of sp2 cluster at various suspensions [12,51].

Fig. 7(b) shows PL excitation spectra of B-GQD-13 suspensions in n-hexane and PGMEA, where four excitation bands at 360–380, 400–420, 450–500, and 530–560 nm can be identified. The analysis of PL spectrum can be very helpful in determining the optimal excitation energy (or maximal wavelength, MW) and the strongest emission range for a phosphor material [47]. As demonstrated in Fig. 7(b), the B-GQD-13 suspension includes similar PL excitation bands, but with different intensities at fixed wavelengths. Fig. 7(c) and (d) demonstrate PL emission spectra of B-GQD-13 suspensions in n-hexane and PGMEA excited at various wavelengths, respectively. According to Fig. 7(c), the B-GQD-13 suspension in n-hexane displays a quasi-symmetric peak at ca. 541 nm with small full-width at half maximum (FWHM, about 31.7 nm) excited at 450 nm. Such a small magnitude of FWHM fulfills the requirements of the state-of-the-art semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., FWHM: 45–50 nm and 36 nm for InP and In(Zn)P quantum dots, respectively) [52]. The analysis of the PL emission spectra also reveals that the B-GQD-13 suspension demonstrates wavelength-independent emission excited under the UV and blue-light illumination. Thus, the FWHM is narrow, while the Stokes displacement is large; such a combination prevents overlapping of the emission and excitation spectrum, and is hugely beneficial for the detection of the fluorescence signal [53]. Fig. S7 depicts the PL emission spectra of the pristine GQD, B-GQD-12, and B-GQD-11 suspensions in n-hexane at different wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S7, the quasi-symmetric peaks center at ca. 541.5 nm with narrow FWHMs within the range of 30.9–36.3 nm. This observation confirms high-quality green fluorescence emitted from all B-GQD suspensions under the UV and blue-light illumination.

It is important to note that the B-GQD-13 suspension in PGMEA exhibits one primary symmetric peak at ca. 617 nm, followed by a secondary peak at 380–400 nm when excited at 360 and 380 nm, as shown in Fig. 7(d). The secondary peak disappears when the suspension is excited under the visible light illumination (i.e., 400–450 nm). Similarly, the B-GQD-13 suspension in PGMEA exhibits a wavelength-independent emission behavior. The FWHM values of the suspension reach as low as 42.7 and 42.5 nm at 360 and 450 nm, respectively. Such narrow FWHM implies that the B-GQD-13 suspension is capable of emitting highly intense red fluorescence. The other samples (i.e., pristine GQD, B-GQD-12, and B-GQD-11 samples) also demonstrate red fluorescence emission centered at 609–617 nm (see Fig. S8). One extra lump at 500–550 nm can also be observed within the PL emission of B-GQD-12 and B-GQD-13 samples. This behavior is most probably due to excessive BC2O and BCO2 species covering the N-doped graphene network of GQDs. The calculation of density functional theory (DFT) has demonstrated that the BC3 plays an important role modulating the electronic transition energy, where the effect of BC2O and BCO2 (i.e., oxidized B bonding configurations) on tuning the electronic and optical properties of GQDs is insignificant (dictated by the hybridization form of carbon) [54]. According to the DFT analysis, the presence of BC2O induces a red shift of absorption spectra, while the absorption spectra of the B-doped GQDs with BCO2 exhibit a blue shift [54]. Indeed, both B-GQD-12 and B-GQD-11 samples contain high boronization level (confirmed by the XPS analysis); thus, twin PL emission peaks due to coexistence of BC2O and BCO2 within the GQD lattices have been emerged as shown in Fig. 7.

Fig. 8 (a) includes the maximal wavelength (MW) along with the FWHM of all B-GQD suspensions in n-hexane and PGMEA under the blue-light illumination. It can be seen that the suspensions illuminate highly intense PL emissions with narrow FWHM, i.e., green in n-hexane and red in PGMEA. This solvent-dependent behavior is very similar to the “solvatochromism” commonly observed in the organic dyes [55–57]. Particularly, the suspension in PGMEA induces an obvious redshift, as compared to that in n-hexane. The red-shifted absorption band (i.e., region (iii)) of B-GQD-13 suspension (see Fig. 7(a)) implies the formation of B-doped GQD–PGMEA complex due to strong interaction between the NO2-modified GQD surface and PGMEA [55]. Such a dynamic interaction results in emitting red fluorescence.

It is also critical to evaluate the PL performance of B-GQDs through assessing the QY value associated with various GQD suspensions. The QY index quantifies the ratio of the number of photon emitted to the number of photon adsorbed during the radiation-induced process [58]. Fig. 8(b) illustrates variation of QY (at 450 nm), considering the chemical ratio of boric acid to TNP, where a single pick can be obtained in the range of 1:3 and 1:2 in w/w (with respect to corresponding chemical rations). The QY values of B-GQD-13 suspension in n-hexane and PGMEA reach as high as 99.8% and 17.1%, respectively. The B-GQD-12 suspension also delivers high green and red emissions, i.e., QY: 90.8% (green) and 16.7% (red), which are much higher than that of the pristine GQD and B-GQD-11 samples. Therefore, it can be concluded that via adjusting the B-dopant concentration and modulating the chemical ratio of boric acid to TNP, the PL intensity of the B-GQDs can be finely tuned and improved as confirmed through the HR-TEM, XPS, and UV–vis studies.

In this work, we demonstrated a novel and efficient solvothermal technique for synthesizing highly luminescent B-doped GQDs. The chemical ratio of the boric acid to TNP precursor was determined as a crucial factor for modulating the B-dopant concentration within the GQD lattices. The suspensions in n-hexane and PGMEA exhibited well dispersion, mainly originated from the NO2 groups formed in the GQD edge sites resulting in high dispersivity in both nonpolar and polar solvents. The presence of boron doping in GQDs was shown to enhance the PL performance as evidenced by ultrahigh QY as well as narrow FWHM values under the blue light illumination. The B-doped GQD suspensions were homogeneously stored in n-hexane and PGMEA, enabling highly fluorescent green and red emissions. The green emission took place at ca. 541 nm with a narrow FWHM of 31.7 nm, whereas the red emission centered at ca. 617 nm with a FWHM of 42.5 nm. The QY values of green and red fluorescence reached as high as 99.8 and 17.1%, respectively. Since all the GQDs possessed an identical particle size distribution, tuning the B-dopant concentration along with adjusting the chemical ratio of the boric acid to TNP were identified as critical knobs for improving the PL intensity of B-GQDs. Accordingly, the novel one-pot solvothermal route developed in this work paves the way for synthesizing B-doped GQDs with tunable optical properties for next-generation biosensors and biomedical/energy devices.

Ruey-Shin Juang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Methodology. Chien-Te Hsieh: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Chih-Peng Kao: Visualization, Investigation. Yasser Ashraf Gandomi: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Validation. Chun-Chieh Fu: Investigation, Data curation. Shou-Hsuan Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Siyong Gu: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (Contact No.: MOST 108-2221-E-155-036-MY3) is greatly acknowledged. Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2H0062 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.
